title: Strait peace pours oil on RBA’s troubled waters
full_text:
A fragile peace deal reached between the US and Iran, and the ensuing fall in wholesale crude prices should reassure the Reserve Bank’s monetary policy board that the worst of the energy shock is over.
Combined with numerous signs that the economy has hit the wall due to interest rate rises, high fuel prices and the tax changes announced in the federal budget, the commitment to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to shipping traffic should give board members more confidence to keep rates on hold.
But while they may now give more weight to the downside risk to economic growth, the inflation battle is far from over.
Stocks soared as oil prices plunged on Monday.
The ASX 200 stock index rose as much as 1.5 per cent to a two-month high of 8937.5, adding to a 2 per cent rise on Friday.
Brent crude oil futures dived 5 per cent to a three-month low of $US83.04 a barrel.
S&P 500 futures rose more than 1 per cent.
But the confidence in markets – boosted by the spectacular debut of Elon Musk’s SpaceX which was backed by billionaire Australian investor Gina Rinehart – isn’t evident on main street.
Consumer and business confidence has plunged, household spending has dived, unemployment is spiking and house prices are dropping – more so since the budget.
The US and Iran reached a pact to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, according to The Wall Street Journal.
For central bankers, lower oil eases the risk of second-round price effects. RBA governor Michele Bullock has said the central bank’s three interest rate increases this year gave the board “space to see how the conflict plays out and the response of Australian households and businesses to the shock”.
With Hormuz set to reopen, that space widens considerably. But while inflation is well above target levels, economic stress builds.
Earlier this month NAB chief executive Andrew Irvine took the unusual step of writing an open letter to customers referring to “challenges facing the country” and saying people felt “increasingly uncertain”.
A week later, NAB chief economist Sally Auld – until recently among the more hawkish voices in the market – switched from predicting further rate rises to expecting an extended pause before interest rate cuts in the second half of 2027.
“We are minded to view the proposed changes to taxation arrangements for housing and other asset classes as an exogenous tightening of financial conditions,” Auld said. “Tighter financial conditions will be reflected in both a slowing in house price growth and housing credit growth.
“Consequently, we have made downward revisions to our forecasts for both of these variables.
“We have greater conviction that the next move in rates is down, but less conviction on the timing.” However she expects core inflation to run above target into the middle of 2027. At the same time, ANZ became the latest bank to cut its housing price forecasts. ANZ now expects capital city housing prices to fall 2.1 per cent this year, and 3.3 per cent in 2027. That compares with its previous forecast of 2.8 per cent growth in 2026 and 2.1 per cent next year.
ANZ said the combination of global uncertainty, 75 basis points of rate rises and recent housing policy changes in the budget have “led to a sharper than expected slowdown in Australia’s housing market”.
“So far, Sydney and Melbourne have been most affected, and we expect dwelling prices in Australia’s two biggest cities to fall about 8 per cent this year,” ANZ economists said.
“But that weakness is spreading across the country, and we are forecasting price falls in all of the capitals next year.” HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham has for months said a fuel price spike would hit the economy fast, limiting but not removing the risk of a self-fulfilling rise in inflation expectations. With growth indicators weakening and house prices now falling, he said the best course was to pause and stay vigilant rather than rush to cut.
Goldman Sachs chief economist Andrew Boak also expects the RBA to keep the cash rate on hold after three quick increases delivered “space” to assess the shock. But like most, he foresees risk of another move later this year on second-round inflation effects, the full extent of which are yet to be seen. With oil falling and a path to reopening Hormuz, that risk appears smaller but hasn’t disappeared entirely if wages and services inflation re accelerate.
RBC senior economist Mary Jo Vergara also expects an on-hold decision, due to restrictive policy, softer data and market pricing, giving the RBA room to wait while it keeps firm language on inflation.
Where does that leave policy? The peace deal and oil’s drop should calm the board’s near-term inflation nerves, if only because it significantly reduces the risk of the RBA’s “adverse scenarios” – whereby Brent crude oil hits $US145 a barrel before gradually falling to the mid to low $US90s by mid-2028. The economy is slowing and budget tax changes are tightening conditions without further rate rises. The board should acknowledge the improving energy backdrop and the weakening pulse in activity, but restate its readiness to act if inflation expectations drift. If the strait reopens and the peace holds, time and lower petrol prices do some of the heavy lifting. But the RBA’s current battle to control inflation is far from over.
